suggestions.name: DateFragmentCalculator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive computational unit focused on calculating time fragments from dates and calendars. They share the same core algorithm and provide different time unit perspectives of the same calculation. Extracting them creates a specialized calculator that encapsulates complex temporal arithmetic logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: calculating time fragments from date/calendar objects. They extract portions of time (days, hours, minutes, seconds, milliseconds) from a given date fragment (year, month, day, etc.) and convert them to specified time units. All methods work together to provide different granularities of the same core calculation.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "time fragment calculation" domain. They represent a cohesive subsystem for breaking down dates into measurable time components. The core logic is centralized in the private getFragment methods, with public methods providing convenient facades for different time units. This is a classic computational responsibility focused on temporal arithmetic.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates fragment calculation logic from other date utilities
- Code Reusability: Fragment calculator can be used independently by other date/time classes
- Complexity Reduction: Removes complex temporal arithmetic from the main DateUtils class
- Testability: Fragment calculations can be tested in isolation with focused test cases
- Maintainability: Changes to fragment calculation logic are contained within one class

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate computational concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all perform fragment calculations) and can achieve low coupling (minimal dependencies on external classes). This extraction supports the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a focused calculator class.

suggestions.name: DateConverter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive conversion unit that transforms legacy Date objects to modern temporal types. They share the same conversion pattern and domain responsibility, making them ideal candidates for extraction into a specialized DateConverter class that bridges legacy and modern date APIs.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, focused responsibility: converting legacy java.util.Date objects to modern Java 8+ temporal types (LocalDateTime, OffsetDateTime, ZonedDateTime). All methods follow the same pattern - they take a Date and optionally a TimeZone, then convert to the target temporal type using the date's instant and zone information.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "date conversion" domain, specifically bridging the gap between legacy and modern Java date/time APIs. They represent a cohesive subsystem for temporal type transformation, with the helper method toZoneId() supporting the conversion process by handling TimeZone to ZoneId mapping.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates date conversion logic from other date utilities
- Code Reusability: Conversion logic can be reused across different modules needing legacy date migration
- Complexity Reduction: Removes conversion complexity from the main DateUtils class, making it more focused
- Testability: Conversion logic can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate conversion concerns. The Converter pattern is appropriate here as these methods transform objects from one type to another without changing the underlying data, creating a specialized component with high cohesion around temporal type conversion.

suggestions.name: DateManipulator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for manipulating Date objects through arithmetic operations, field assignments, and string parsing. Extracting them into a DateManipulator class improves code organization by centralizing all date transformation logic and reduces the complexity of the original DateUtils class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of manipulating Date objects by either adding/subtracting time amounts, setting specific time field values, or parsing string representations into Date objects. They collectively provide comprehensive date manipulation capabilities including arithmetic operations (add methods), field assignment operations (set methods), and string-to-date conversion operations (parse methods).

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "date manipulation" domain. They represent a cohesive subsystem for transforming Date objects through various operations - temporal arithmetic, field modification, and string parsing. The methods share common patterns: they all work with Date objects as input/output, use Calendar internally for operations, and follow consistent naming conventions (addX, setX, parseX).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates all date manipulation logic into one focused class
- Code Reusability: Date manipulation operations can be easily reused across different contexts
- Complexity Reduction: Removes a large cluster of related methods from the original DateUtils class
- Testability: Date manipulation logic can be tested independently with focused test cases
- Maintainability: Changes to date manipulation behavior are centralized in one location

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate date manipulation concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all manipulate dates in various ways) and can operate with low coupling (minimal dependencies on external classes). This extraction creates a specialized component following the Single Responsibility Principle.
